pattern_recognition: Matrix Search Problem - This problem falls under the category of searching in a 2D matrix where both rows and columns are sorted. The sorted nature of the matrix allows for more efficient searching techniques than a naive approach. Signals include the need for optimization in search algorithms due to potentially large matrix sizes.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check every element in the matrix to see if it matches the target.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through each row and each column, comparing each element to the target.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Utilize the sorted properties of the matrix to eliminate rows or columns based on comparisons with the target.
approaches.algorithm: Start from the top-right corner of the matrix and move left or down based on comparisons with the target.

dry_run: Consider the matrix [[1, 4, 7, 11], [2, 5, 8, 12], [3, 6, 9, 16], [10, 13, 14, 17]] and target 5. Start at (0, 3) which is 11. Since 11 > 5, move left to (0, 2) which is 7. Again, 7 > 5, move left to (0, 1) which is 4. Now, 4 < 5, move down to (1, 1) which is 5. Found the target.
edge_cases: Empty matrix should return false. A single element matrix should return true if the element matches the target. Duplicates in the matrix should still return true if the target exists. Large matrices should be handled efficiently without timeouts. Overflow is not a concern as we are only checking for existence.
common_pitfalls: 1) Confusing row and column indices when navigating the matrix. Correctly identify the starting point. 2) Not utilizing the sorted properties effectively, leading to unnecessary checks. 3) Failing to handle edge cases like empty matrices or single-element matrices properly.
